competitors.label: MDN Closures
competitors.selectionReason: This is the strongest canonical reference for closures and one of the first pages candidates are likely to compare against.
theirStrengths: It combines closure basics, practical patterns, loop traps, and performance notes in one authoritative page.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly framed as a production-debug answer than our shipped trivia.

realWorldUseCases: MDN already covers practical use cases such as module-style private state and the classic loop-capture failure, so the real-world gap is narrow.
actionableExamples: MDN gives more code examples than our current trivia, including loop mistakes and modern fixes.
followUpConfusion: MDN also answers the usual follow-ups around shared loop bindings and performance implications more comprehensively.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the stale-state and memory-retention framing, but add one explicit production callback scenario so the practical angle is undeniably stronger.
actionableExamples: Add one compact before/after closure bug example that starts from a broken async callback and ends with the corrected binding.
followUpConfusion: Add one short follow-up block on when closures keep old objects alive through listeners or timers.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Closures
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant quiz answer for the same interview question, so it is the strongest interview-prep calibration point.
theirStrengths: It is tightly aligned with the interview phrasing and includes concrete patterns like private variables and counters.
theirGaps: It is lighter on stale async state and memory-retention language than our page.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd connects closures to private variables and practical usage, so the page is already grounded in real scenarios.
actionableExamples: Its counter-style and private-variable examples keep the example quality competitive with ours.
followUpConfusion: It answers the definition, use case, and why-you-would-use-one follow-up directly enough that the current comparison stays close.

realWorldUseCases: Add one UI-specific stale closure example so our page has a clearer frontend-debug advantage.
actionableExamples: Include one worked example that contrasts a useful closure with a broken loop-capture closure.
followUpConfusion: Add one explicit note about long-lived listeners retaining outdated objects or state.

competitors.label: DigitalOcean Closures
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial-style page is a strong mainstream reference competitor that explains closures through callbacks and asynchronous code.
competitors.winReason: Our page is more explicit about frontend bug patterns and the likely next confusion, while DigitalOcean stays more introductory.
theirStrengths: It gives accessible explanations of callbacks, async execution, and lexical scope.
theirGaps: It stays introductory and is lighter on stale-state and memory-retention traps.

realWorldUseCases: That powers private state, but the production pitfalls are loop-capture bugs, stale async state, and memory retention when callbacks keep old data alive.
followUpConfusion: A closure means a function remembers variables from the place where it was created, not from where it is called.

realWorldUseCases: DigitalOcean covers callbacks and async execution, but it does not lean as hard into production bug framing.
actionableExamples: Its examples are concrete enough that the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on the likely next confusion around loop bindings and long-lived references.

actionableExamples: Add one more compact factory example so the page is strong on both bug-first framing and legitimate usage.
